data.content:
### YAML Example for Multi-file Skill

Source: https://code.claude.com/docs/en/skills

YAML frontmatter for a multi-file Claude Skill named 'pdf-processing'. It details the skill's functionality, usage context, and required packages (pypdf, pdfplumber).

```yaml
---
name: pdf-processing
description: Extract text, fill forms, merge PDFs. Use when working with PDF files, forms, or document extraction. Requires pypdf and pdfplumber packages.
---

# PDF Processing

```

--------------------------------

### YAML Example for Simple Skill

Source: https://code.claude.com/docs/en/skills

YAML frontmatter for a simple Claude Skill stored in a single SKILL.md file. It includes the skill's name, a descriptive purpose, and usage instructions.

```yaml
---
name: generating-commit-messages
description: Generates clear commit messages from git diffs. Use when writing commit messages or reviewing staged changes.
---

# Generating Commit Messages

## Instructions

1. Run `git diff --staged` to see changes
2. I'll suggest a commit message with:
   - Summary under 50 characters
   - Detailed description
   - Affected components

## Best practices

- Use present tense
- Explain what and why, not how
```

--------------------------------

### YAML Example for Skill with Tool Permissions

Source: https://code.claude.com/docs/en/skills

YAML frontmatter for a Claude Skill that requires specific tool permissions (Read, Grep, Glob). It outlines the skill's purpose, allowed tools, and a review checklist.

```yaml
---
name: code-reviewer
description: Review code for best practices and potential issues. Use when reviewing code, checking PRs, or analyzing code quality.
allowed-tools: Read, Grep, Glob
---

# Code Reviewer

## Review checklist

1. Code organization and structure
2. Error handling
3. Performance considerations
4. Security concerns
5. Test coverage

## Instructions

1. Read the target files using Read tool
2. Search for patterns using Grep
3. Find related files using Glob
4. Provide detailed feedback on code quality
```

--------------------------------

### Bash Commands for Skill Troubleshooting

Source: https://code.claude.com/docs/en/skills

A collection of Bash commands useful for troubleshooting Claude Skills. This includes checking the frontmatter of SKILL.md files, validating YAML syntax, and listing skill file locations.

```bash
# View frontmatter
cat .claude/skills/my-skill/SKILL.md | head -n 15

# Check for common issues
# - Missing opening or closing ---
# - Tabs instead of spaces
# - Unquoted strings with special characters
```

```bash
# Personal Skills
ls ~/.claude/skills/*/SKILL.md

# Project Skills
ls .claude/skills/*/SKILL.md
```

--------------------------------

### YAML Snippet for Specific Skill Descriptions

Source: https://code.claude.com/docs/en/skills

Illustrates how to write specific descriptions for multiple Claude Skills to avoid conflicts. Each description highlights distinct functionalities and use cases using targeted keywords.

```yaml
# Skill 1
description: Analyze sales data in Excel files and CRM exports. Use for sales reports, pipeline analysis, and revenue tracking.

# Skill 2
description: Analyze log files and system metrics data. Use for performance monitoring, debugging, and system diagnostics.
```

--------------------------------

### YAML Skill Description Example

Source: https://code.claude.com/docs/en/skills

Example of a clear YAML description for a Claude Skill, specifying its functionality and intended use cases. This helps Claude discover and activate the skill appropriately.

```yaml
description: Analyze Excel spreadsheets, create pivot tables, and generate charts. Use when working with Excel files, spreadsheets, or analyzing tabular data in .xlsx format.
```

--------------------------------

### Bash Command to Set Execute Permissions

Source: https://code.claude.com/docs/en/skills

Bash command to set execute permissions for Python scripts within a Claude Skill. This ensures that scripts can be run by the system when the skill is invoked.

```bash
chmod +x .claude/skills/my-skill/scripts/*.py
```

--------------------------------

### Markdown Skill Version History Example

Source: https://code.claude.com/docs/en/skills

Example of documenting Skill versions in Markdown format, using a version history section. This allows tracking changes, including breaking changes and new features, across different releases.

```markdown
# My Skill

## Version History
- v2.0.0 (2025-10-01): Breaking changes to API
- v1.1.0 (2025-09-15): Added new features
- v1.0.0 (2025-09-01): Initial release
```

--------------------------------

### File-based Subagent Configuration (Markdown)

Source: https://code.claude.com/docs/en/sub-agents

Defines subagents within Markdown files using a YAML frontmatter structure. This method allows for detailed configuration of name, description, tools, model, permissionMode, and skills, followed by the system prompt. It's suitable for persistent or complex subagent definitions.

```markdown
---
name: your-sub-agent-name
description: Description of when this subagent should be invoked
tools: tool1, tool2, tool3  # Optional - inherits all tools if omitted
model: sonnet  # Optional - specify model alias or 'inherit'
permissionMode: default  # Optional - permission mode for the subagent
skills: skill1, skill2  # Optional - skills to auto-load
---

Your subagent's system prompt goes here. This can be multiple paragraphs
and should clearly define the subagent's role, capabilities, and approach
to solving problems.

Include specific instructions, best practices, and any constraints
the subagent should follow.
```

--------------------------------

### Ask Claude Code About its Capabilities

Source: https://code.claude.com/docs/en/quickstart

Inquires about the general functionalities and capabilities of Claude Code. This is useful for understanding what tasks the AI can assist with.

```text
> what can Claude Code do?
```

--------------------------------

### Configure Hooks for Tool Execution (JSON)

Source: https://code.claude.com/docs/en/settings

Enables the configuration of custom commands to be executed before or after tool executions. This setting allows for pre-execution setup or post-execution cleanup actions using shell commands.

```json
`{"PreToolUse": {"Bash": "echo 'Running command...'"}}`
```

--------------------------------

### Query Claude about pull request creation capabilities

Source: https://code.claude.com/docs/en/common-workflows

Example of asking Claude about its built-in capabilities and features. Users can query Claude Code's documentation to learn about specific functionality like pull request creation support.

```text
> can Claude Code create pull requests?
```

--------------------------------

### PostToolUse Hook: JSON for Feedback and Context

Source: https://code.claude.com/docs/en/hooks

Facilitates PostToolUse hooks to provide feedback after tool execution using JSON. Supports blocking the tool call with a reason or adding additional context for Claude to consider without blocking.

```json
{
  "decision": "block",
  "reason": "Explanation for decision",
  "hookSpecificOutput": {
    "hookEventName": "PostToolUse",
    "additionalContext": "Additional information for Claude"
  }
}
```

--------------------------------

### PreToolUse Hook: Control Tool Call Execution with JSON

Source: https://code.claude.com/docs/en/hooks

Enables PreToolUse hooks to manage tool call permissions and modify inputs using JSON. Supports allowing, denying, or asking for confirmation of tool calls, along with providing reasons and updated input parameters.

```json
{
  "hookSpecificOutput": {
    "hookEventName": "PreToolUse",
    "permissionDecision": "allow",
    "permissionDecisionReason": "My reason here",
    "updatedInput": {
      "field_to_modify": "new value"
    }
  }
}
```

--------------------------------

### Query Claude about available slash commands

Source: https://code.claude.com/docs/en/common-workflows

Example of asking Claude to list and explain available slash commands. Users can discover all built-in and personal commands through this query.

```text
> what slash commands are available?
```

--------------------------------

### Add Claude to Build Script for Linting

Source: https://code.claude.com/docs/en/common-workflows

Integrate Claude Code into your project's build process by adding a script to your package.json. This allows Claude to act as a linter, checking for specific issues like typos in code changes compared to a main branch.

```json
{
    ...
    "scripts": {
        ...
        "lint:claude": "claude -p 'you are a linter. please look at the changes vs. main and report any issues related to typos. report the filename and line number on one line, and a description of the issue on the second line. do not return any other text.'"
    }
}

```

--------------------------------

### Hook Output: Exit Codes and Error Handling

Source: https://code.claude.com/docs/en/hooks

Comprehensive reference for hook output mechanisms using exit codes, stdout, and stderr. Hooks communicate status through exit codes (0 for success, 2 for blocking errors, other for non-blocking errors) and can return JSON output for structured control.

```APIDOC
## Hook Output Mechanisms

### Overview
Hooks communicate status and feedback through exit codes, stdout, and stderr. Two mutually-exclusive output methods are supported: simple exit codes and advanced JSON output.

### Exit Code Behaviors

#### Exit Code 0: Success
- **Behavior**: Execution continues normally
- **stdout**: Shown to user in verbose mode (ctrl+o)
- **Exception**: For `UserPromptSubmit` and `SessionStart` hooks, stdout is added to Claude's context instead
- **JSON Processing**: JSON output in stdout is parsed for structured control

#### Exit Code 2: Blocking Error
- **Behavior**: Blocks the operation (behavior varies by hook type)
- **stdout**: JSON in stdout is NOT processed
- **stderr**: Used as the error message, formatted as `[command]: {stderr}`
- **Feedback**: Error message fed back to Claude (or shown to user only for certain hooks)

#### Other Exit Codes: Non-Blocking Error
- **Behavior**: Execution continues
- **stderr**: Shown to user in verbose mode with format `Failed with non-blocking status code: {stderr}`
- **Default Message**: If stderr is empty, shows `No stderr output`

### Per-Hook-Event Exit Code 2 Behavior

| Hook Event | Behavior |
|---|---|
| PreToolUse | Blocks tool call, shows stderr to Claude |
| PostToolUse | Shows stderr to Claude (tool already executed) |
| Notification | Shows stderr to user only |
| UserPromptSubmit | Blocks prompt processing, erases prompt, shows stderr to user only |
| Stop | Blocks stoppage, shows stderr to Claude |
| SubagentStop | Blocks stoppage, shows stderr to Claude subagent |
| PreCompact | Shows stderr to user only |
| SessionStart | Shows stderr to user only |
| SessionEnd | Shows stderr to user only |

### Output Examples

#### Successful Hook (Exit Code 0)
```bash
exit 0
echo "Hook executed successfully"
```

#### Blocking Error (Exit Code 2)
```bash
exit 2
echo "Permission denied: Operation not allowed" >&2
```

#### Non-Blocking Error (Exit Code 1)
```bash
exit 1
echo "Warning: Operation completed with issues" >&2
```
```

--------------------------------

### Configure Allowed Tools with --allowedTools Flag

Source: https://code.claude.com/docs/en/cli-reference

Specify a list of tools that Claude Code can use without prompting for user permission. These tools are allowed in addition to any configured in settings.json files.

```bash
claude --allowedTools "Bash(git log:*)" "Bash(git diff:*)" "Read"
```

--------------------------------

### PostToolUse Input Schema

Source: https://code.claude.com/docs/en/hooks

Defines the expected JSON structure for the PostToolUse hook, including session details, tool input, and tool response. This schema is used to provide context after a tool has been used.

```json
{
  "session_id": "abc123",
  "transcript_path": "/Users/.../.claude/projects/.../00893aaf-19fa-41d2-8238-13269b9b3ca0.jsonl",
  "cwd": "/Users/...",
  "permission_mode": "default",
  "hook_event_name": "PostToolUse",
  "tool_name": "Write",
  "tool_input": {
    "file_path": "/path/to/file.txt",
    "content": "file content"
  },
  "tool_response": {
    "filePath": "/path/to/file.txt",
    "success": true
  },
  "tool_use_id": "toolu_01ABC123..."
}
```

--------------------------------

### PermissionRequest Hook: JSON for Decision and Input Modification

Source: https://code.claude.com/docs/en/hooks

Allows PermissionRequest hooks to control permission requests with JSON output. It can allow or deny requests, optionally modifying tool inputs when allowed or providing a denial message and interrupt flag when denied.

```json
{
  "hookSpecificOutput": {
    "hookEventName": "PermissionRequest",
    "decision": {
      "behavior": "allow",
      "updatedInput": {
        "command": "npm run lint"
      }
    }
  }
}
```

--------------------------------

### Auto-Approve Documentation File Reads in Python

Source: https://code.claude.com/docs/en/hooks

This hook script reads JSON input from stdin containing tool information and automatically approves file read operations for documentation files (.md, .mdx, .txt, .json). It returns a JSON decision with approval status and optional output suppression flag. Non-documentation files proceed through normal permission flow.

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python3
import json
import sys

# Load input from stdin
try:
    input_data = json.load(sys.stdin)
except json.JSONDecodeError as e:
    print(f"Error: Invalid JSON input: {e}", file=sys.stderr)
    sys.exit(1)

tool_name = input_data.get("tool_name", "")
tool_input = input_data.get("tool_input", {})

# Example: Auto-approve file reads for documentation files
if tool_name == "Read":
    file_path = tool_input.get("file_path", "")
    if file_path.endswith((".md", ".mdx", ".txt", ".json")):
        # Use JSON output to auto-approve the tool call
        output = {
            "decision": "approve",
            "reason": "Documentation file auto-approved",
            "suppressOutput": True
        }
        print(json.dumps(output))
        sys.exit(0)

# For other cases, let the normal permission flow proceed
sys.exit(0)
```

--------------------------------

### Format Markdown Files with Language Detection (Python)

Source: https://code.claude.com/docs/en/hooks-guide

This Python script automatically detects and adds language tags to unlabeled code fences in markdown files. It also fixes excessive blank lines. The script processes .md and .mdx files and requires no external dependencies beyond standard Python libraries.

```json
{
  "hooks": {
    "PostToolUse": [
      {
        "matcher": "Edit|Write",
        "hooks": [
          {
            "type": "command",
            "command": "\"$CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR\"/.claude/hooks/markdown_formatter.py"
          }
        ]
      }
    ]
  }
}
```

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python3
"""
Markdown formatter for Claude Code output.
Fixes missing language tags and spacing issues while preserving code content.
"""
import json
import sys
import re
import os

def detect_language(code):
    """Best-effort language detection from code content."""
    s = code.strip()
    
    # JSON detection
    if re.search(r'^\s*[\{\[]', s):
        try:
            json.loads(s)
            return 'json'
        except:
            pass
    
    # Python detection
    if re.search(r'^\s*def\s+\w+\s*\(', s, re.M) or \
       re.search(r'^\s*(import|from)\s+\w+', s, re.M):
        return 'python'
    
    # JavaScript detection  
    if re.search(r'\b(function\s+\w+\s*\(|const\s+\w+\s*=)', s) or \
       re.search(r'=>|console\.(log|error)', s):
        return 'javascript'
    
    # Bash detection
    if re.search(r'^#!.*\b(bash|sh)\b', s, re.M) or \
       re.search(r'\b(if|then|fi|for|in|do|done)\b', s):
        return 'bash'
    
    # SQL detection
    if re.search(r'\b(SELECT|INSERT|UPDATE|DELETE|CREATE)\s+', s, re.I):
        return 'sql'
        
    return 'text'

def format_markdown(content):
    """Format markdown content with language detection."""
    # Fix unlabeled code fences
    def add_lang_to_fence(match):
        indent, info, body, closing = match.groups()
        if not info.strip():
            lang = detect_language(body)
            return f"{indent}```{lang}\n{body}{closing}\n"
        return match.group(0)
    
    fence_pattern = r'(?ms)^([ \t]{0,3})```([^\n]*)\n(.*?)(\n\1```)\s*$'
    content = re.sub(fence_pattern, add_lang_to_fence, content)
    
    # Fix excessive blank lines (only outside code fences)
    content = re.sub(r'\n{3,}', '\n\n', content)
    
    return content.rstrip() + '\n'

# Main execution
try:
    input_data = json.load(sys.stdin)
    file_path = input_data.get('tool_input', {}).get('file_path', '')
    
    if not file_path.endswith(('.md', '.mdx')):
        sys.exit(0)  # Not a markdown file
    
    if os.path.exists(file_path):
        with open(file_path, 'r', encoding='utf-8') as f:
            content = f.read()
        
        formatted = format_markdown(content)
        
        if formatted != content:
            with open(file_path, 'w', encoding='utf-8') as f:
                f.write(formatted)
            print(f"✓ Fixed markdown formatting in {file_path}")
    
except Exception as e:
    print(f"Error formatting markdown: {e}", file=sys.stderr)
    sys.exit(1)

```

```bash
chmod +x .claude/hooks/markdown_formatter.py
```

--------------------------------

### Detect and Block Sensitive Patterns in Python

Source: https://code.claude.com/docs/en/hooks

This snippet checks user input against regular expression patterns to identify sensitive information like passwords, secrets, keys, and tokens. When a match is found, it outputs a JSON decision to block the prompt with a specific security violation reason. The script uses sys.exit(0) to prevent further execution.

```python
import re
import json
import sys
import datetime

# Check for sensitive patterns
sensitive_patterns = [
    (r"(?i)\b(password|secret|key|token)\s*[:=]", "Prompt contains potential secrets"),
]

for pattern, message in sensitive_patterns:
    if re.search(pattern, prompt):
        # Use JSON output to block with a specific reason
        output = {
            "decision": "block",
            "reason": f"Security policy violation: {message}. Please rephrase your request without sensitive information."
        }
        print(json.dumps(output))
        sys.exit(0)

# Add current time to context
context = f"Current time: {datetime.datetime.now()}"
print(context)

# Allow the prompt to proceed with the additional context
sys.exit(0)
```

--------------------------------

### Multi-turn Legal Assistant with Session Persistence

Source: https://code.claude.com/docs/en/headless

Legal document review agent using session management to maintain context across multiple turns. Persists session ID to resume conversation for contract review, compliance checks, and risk analysis.

```bash
# Legal document review with session persistence
session_id=$(claude -p "Start legal review session" --output-format json | jq -r '.session_id')

# Review contract in multiple steps
claude -p --resume "$session_id" "Review contract.pdf for liability clauses"
claude -p --resume "$session_id" "Check compliance with GDPR requirements"
claude -p --resume "$session_id" "Generate executive summary of risks"
```

--------------------------------

### Hook Input Schema with Common Fields

Source: https://code.claude.com/docs/en/hooks

Standard JSON structure for hook input data received via stdin by all hook types. Contains common fields like session_id, transcript_path, current working directory, and permission mode, with event-specific fields added based on hook type.

```typescript
{
  // Common fields
  session_id: string
  transcript_path: string  // Path to conversation JSON
  cwd: string              // The current working directory when the hook is invoked
  permission_mode: string  // Current permission mode: "default", "plan", "acceptEdits", or "bypassPermissions"

  // Event-specific fields
  hook_event_name: string
  ...
}
```

--------------------------------

### Configure Disallowed Tools with --disallowedTools Flag

Source: https://code.claude.com/docs/en/cli-reference

Specify a list of tools that Claude Code cannot use without prompting for user permission. These restrictions apply in addition to any configured in settings.json files.

```bash
claude --disallowedTools "Bash(git log:*)" "Bash(git diff:*)" "Edit"
```

--------------------------------

### Claude Code Action with Slash Commands

Source: https://code.claude.com/docs/en/github-actions

This workflow demonstrates using Claude Code Action with a specific prompt, '/review', triggered by pull request events. It configures the action to use a maximum of 5 turns.

```yaml
```yaml  theme={null}
name: Code Review
on:
  pull_request:
    types: [opened, synchronize]
jobs:
  review:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: anthropics/claude-code-action@v1
        with:
          anthropic_api_key: ${{ secrets.ANTHROPIC_API_KEY }}
          prompt: "/review"
          claude_args: "--max-turns 5"
```
```

--------------------------------

### Configure Hooks for MCP Tools in JSON

Source: https://code.claude.com/docs/en/hooks

This JSON configuration demonstrates how to set up Claude Code hooks targeting Model Context Protocol (MCP) tools using naming patterns. It includes examples for logging memory operations and validating write operations across MCP servers using regex matchers for flexible tool targeting.

```json
{
  "hooks": {
    "PreToolUse": [
      {
        "matcher": "mcp__memory__.*",
        "hooks": [
          {
            "type": "command",
            "command": "echo 'Memory operation initiated' >> ~/mcp-operations.log"
          }
        ]
      },
      {
        "matcher": "mcp__.*__write.*",
        "hooks": [
          {
            "type": "command",
            "command": "/home/user/scripts/validate-mcp-write.py"
          }
        ]
      }
    ]
  }
}
```

--------------------------------

### Claude Code Action Advanced Configuration

Source: https://code.claude.com/docs/en/github-actions

This YAML snippet demonstrates advanced configuration for the Claude Code Action, specifically focusing on passing CLI arguments to the Claude model. It shows how to set parameters like maximum conversation turns and the specific model to use.

```yaml
claude_args: "--max-turns 5 --model claude-sonnet-4-5-20250929 --mcp-config /path/to/config.json"
```
